New Chinese Open Claw is insane. So, China just dropped something absolutely insane. It's called Pico Claw. It's a brand new open-source AI agent, and it can run on a tiny little board the size of your thumb or even an old phone. You forgot in a drawer. This thing is free. It's open source, and it might change everything about how AI agents work. Let me break it all down for you right now. So, a company out of Shenzhen just released this thing. Shenzhen is basically the tech capital of China. Everything crazy in tech comes out of there. The company is called Sepeed. They've been building chips and AI tools since 2011, so they know what they're doing. Now, here's what makes Piclaw absolutely nuts. OpenClaw needs about 430,000 lines of code to work. Pico Claw does the same core job in just 4,000 lines. That's not a typo. 4,000. Imagine taking a whole encyclopedia and shrinking it into a single page. And that single page still does the job. That's what happened here. OpenClaw is built on Typescript and Node.js. Pico claw is built on a language called Go. And Go is like the stripped down race car version of code. No extra weight, no fluff. It just flies. And the startup speed is wild. Open claw can take hundreds of seconds to boot up. Pico Claw starts in less than 1 second. Less than 1 second. You tap a button and it's already ready. No loading, no waiting, just instant. Now, let me clear up some stuff people are getting very wrong on the internet right now because there's a ton of confusion floating around. First big myth. People think Pico Claw runs the AI on that tiny board. No, not even close. The board is just a traffic controller. Think of it like a little mailman. It picks up your message from Telegram or Discord, sends it to the cloud where the real AI brain lives, like Claude or Open AI. Then it brings the answer back to you. The thinking happens in the cloud. The board just passes notes back and forth. That's why it can be so small. Second myth. People think you need some massive powerful computer to run any AI agent. Also not true. Pico Claw runs on any cheap Linux board. A Raspberry Pi works. An old Android phone running Termox works. Even some dusty old gadget sitting in your closet works. No excuses anymore. So, how does this thing actually work under the hood? Both OpenClaw and Pico Claw run on the same basic loop. Here's the simple version. You send a message, the agent reads it, it pulls together all the context, your memory file, your conversation history, your tool list, then it fires all of that to the AI model in the cloud. The AI decides what tools to use. Maybe it reads a Google sheet, maybe it searches the web, it runs those tools, gets the results back, and then either calls more tools or gives you the final answer. That whole cycle is one loop, and it keeps looping until the job is done. Both agents use that exact same loop. The only difference is how much stuff is wrapped around it. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every comment, so make sure you comment below. Okay, so let me paint a picture for you. Imagine you hire a brand new assistant. You hand them a sticky note with a task on it. They read it. They think about it. They pick up the phone and make a call to get some info. They think again, maybe make another call. Then they walk over to your desk and tell you what they found. That whole process from sticky note to answer is one loop. Pico claw and open claw both do that exact thing. Same loop, same sticky note process. The only difference is Piclaw's assistant sits at a tiny folding table. Open Claw's assistant has a full corner office with a filing cabinet and a printer and a coffee machine. Another way to see it, OpenClaw is like building a whole kitchen just to make one cup of coffee. Pico Claw is grabbing a kettle and a mug. Same coffee at the end, way less stuff around it. Now, here's something that's going to bake your brain. 95% of Picl Claw's code was written by an AI agent. Let that sink in. An AI literally rebuilt itself into a lighter and faster version. The AI made us smaller for AI. We're living in the future right now. So, when should you use PC claw and when should you stick with OpenClaw? Let me break this down real simple. Use Poclaw if you want a basic chat assistant on Telegram or Discord. If you want to tinker with AI agents and learn how they work. If you need simple reminders or quick Q&amp;A or if you got some old hardware collecting dust and you want to put it to work, Pico Claw is perfect for all of that. Use OpenClaw if you're running serious business automations. If you need browser control to operate software, if you're working with CRM tools, if you need multi-step workflows where one tool calls another tool, calls another tool, or if you want the full skill marketplace and all the community integrations, OpenClaw is the beast for that. Think of Piclaw as a scalpel. Small, sharp, one clean cut. And OpenClaw is the whole factory. Bigger, louder, way more machines, way more power. Now, if you're serious about using AI agents like Pico Claw and OpenClaw to automate your workflows, and scale your business, you need to check out my AI profit boardroom. It's the best place to learn how to set up these agents, build real automations, and get more customers using AI. I'll drop the link in the description and the comments. Go check it out. Okay, so let's talk about the elephant in the room, the China question. Because I know a lot of you are thinking it, is my data safe? Is it going to China? Here's the real answer. Pico Claw is fully open source. The code is on GitHub. Anyone can read it. Anyone can audit every single line. The agent runs on your hardware. It does not send anything back to CPED servers. And here's the part everyone misses. Your data goes wherever your AI provider is. If you point Pico claw at Claude, your info goes to Anthropic. If you point it at OpenAI, it goes to OpenAI. CPED is nowhere in that chain. The board is just a middleman. Worrying about the board stealing your data is like worrying your keyboard manufacturer is reading your emails. They're not. They're just passing along keystrokes. Now, I want to keep it real with you. Picolaw is early stage. They literally warn you on the page not to use it in production yet. The community is way smaller than OpenClaw Piclaw's GitHub repo. So stay sharp. Do your own homework. With big power comes big risk. But check this out. The GitHub repo pulled in 12,000 stars in just 7 days. That's the fastest growing AI agent repo anyone has seen in a long time. People are fired up about this and for good reason. The community right now is split into three camps. Camp one is the hype crowd. They're losing their minds that a full AI agent loop can run on a board the size of a stamp. And honestly, it is impressive. Camp two is the skeptics. They're saying it's basically just an API client. The board still needs the cloud to do any real thinking. and they're right about that too. But Camp 3 is where I live. The builders, the ones who say, "Look, none of this matters until you actually build automations that move your business forward. The tool is just the vehicle. The automation is the destination. Doesn't matter if your car is a Ferrari or a go-kart. What matters is you're driving somewhere." And here's the thing, nobody is talking about data sovereignty. Everyone is obsessed with making the orchestrator smaller and leaner. But nobody is asking the real question. We're handing our ideas, our prompts, our business plans to these AI models every single day. Where does all that go? Who owns it? That's the conversation we should be having. Now, the smartest setup in my opinion is to use both. Run Pico Claw on a tiny board for your simple daily stuff, quick check-ins, basic reminders, simple chron jobs, web searches, the lightweight tasks, and keep open running on a bigger setup for the heavy lifting, browser automation, complex research, multi-step tool chaining. That hybrid approach is the sweet spot.
